SPIAT3D 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • Basics of the SpatialExperiment object
    • Cell colocalization metrics
    • Clustering algorithms
    • Distance based metrics
    • Spatial heterogeneity metrics

Distance based metrics

Source: vignettes/distance_based_metrics.Rmd
distance_based_metrics.Rmd
library(SPIAT3D)

Introduction

SPIAT3D has two distance based metrics that have been adapted from the original SPIAT. This vignette will go through both of them. Firstly, you will need 3D spatial data to analyse. I will be using a simulated dataset available in this package.

# Get simulated SpatialExperiment object to use as an example for analysis
simulated_spe <- readRDS(system.file("extdata", "simulated_spe.RDS", package = "SPIAT3D"))

print(simulated_spe)
#> Loading required namespace: SpatialExperiment
#> class: SpatialExperiment 
#> dim: 0 10000 
#> metadata(1): simulation
#> assays(1): ''
#> rownames: NULL
#> rowData names(0):
#> colnames: NULL
#> colData names(3): Cell.Type Cell.ID sample_id
#> reducedDimNames(0):
#> mainExpName: NULL
#> altExpNames(0):
#> spatialCoords names(3) : Cell.X.Position Cell.Y.Position
#>   Cell.Z.Position
#> imgData names(1): sample_id

plot_cells3D(simulated_spe,
             plot_cell_types = c("Tumour", "Immune", "Endothelial", "Others"),
             plot_colours = c("orange", "skyblue", "tomato", "lightgray"),
             feature_colname = "Cell.Type")

Pairwise distance between cells

The first distance based metric is the ‘pairwise distance between cells’ metric which involves computing and tabulating the all distances between all cell types that the user is interested in. This outputs a distance matrix, showing the distance for each cell-cell pair.

Be warned when using this function, its very storage and time demanding, and it may not be suitable for use for large datasets.

# Calculate pairwise distance between cells
pairwise_distances <- calculate_pairwise_distances_between_cell_types3D(
    spe = simulated_spe,
    cell_types_of_interest = c("Tumour", "Immune"),
    feature_colname = "Cell.Type",
    show_summary = FALSE,
    plot_image = FALSE
)

Minimum distance between cells

The second distance based metric is the ‘minimum distance between cells’ metric which involves computing and tabulating only the minimum distances between the cell types which the user is interested in. This outputs a similar, but smaller distance matrix compared to the pairwise distance between cells function, showing the minimum distance for each cell-cell pair.

# Calculate minimum distance between cells
minimum_distances <- calculate_minimum_distances_between_cell_types3D(
    spe = simulated_spe,
    cell_types_of_interest = NULL,
    feature_colname = "Cell.Type",
    show_summary = FALSE,
    plot_image = FALSE
)

Interpretating the distance matrices

After obtaining the distance matrices from either the pairwise or minimum distance functions, SPIAT3D offers ways to interpret the data. This includes printing a summary of the distance data, showing the mean, median, … for each cell-cell pair. This also includes plotting the distance data for each cell-cell pair as violin plots.

# Print summary of pairwise distance data
distances_summary <- summarise_distances_between_cell_types3D(
    distances_df = pairwise_distances
)
print(distances_summary)
#>            pair     mean       min      max   median  std_dev reference target
#> 1 Immune/Immune 50.93556 1.1111424 143.8472 50.37245 22.78010    Immune Immune
#> 2 Tumour/Immune 49.36338 0.5010763 149.4711 48.71339 22.44736    Tumour Immune
#> 3 Tumour/Tumour 47.71634 0.6332032 151.2968 46.80121 22.22545    Tumour Tumour

# Plot violin plots of minimum distance data
fig <- plot_distances_between_cell_types_violin3D(
    distances_df = minimum_distances,
    scales = "free_x"
)
#> Plots show mean ± sd
methods::show(fig)

Further analysis of distance matrices

For our own spatial analysis, we found that it was a good idea to represent the 3D spatial data using a single value, rather than a large matrix of values. How you choose to interpret the distance matrices is up to you.

What we adopted to use was the average minimum distance between cells (AMD). We ignored pairwise distance as this was a bit too slow to run. Calculating AMD on several 3D datasets gives you a quantitative way to compare the datasets.

# Get Tumour/Immune AMD
AMD <- distances_summary$mean[distances_summary$pair == "Tumour/Immune"]
print(AMD)
#> [1] 49.36338

Developed by David Le.
